items.q: Does adding a catalyst change the value of Kc?
items.correct: No — a catalyst speeds up forward and reverse reactions equally, so Kc is unchanged.
items.misconception: A catalyst increases Kc by favouring product formation.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst decreases Kc by stabilising the reactants.
items.why: Students think catalysts 'help the reaction' so they must increase Kc; missing that Kc is determined by thermodynamics (ΔG), not by rate.

items.q: What is the difference between a strong acid and a concentrated acid?
items.correct: A strong acid fully dissociates in water; a concentrated acid has a high amount of acid per volume — these are independent properties.
items.misconception: Strong and concentrated mean the same thing for acids.
items.random_wrong: A strong acid is one that has been heated to higher temperature.
items.why: In everyday language 'strong' and 'concentrated' are synonyms (e.g. strong coffee), so students transfer that meaning to chemistry.

items.q: Which method separates an insoluble solid from a liquid?
items.why: Correct answer: Filtration. “Distillation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What is left in the filter paper called?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Residue. “Filtrate” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: If a mixture of 30 g sand + 20 g salt is filtered then evaporated, how many grams of salt should you recover (assuming no losses)?
items.why: Exact answer: 20. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
